$comment: D-MIR-ELEMENT-CONST-ARRAY-GLOBAL-CLASSIFICATION: a file-scope array of const pointers in the DIRECT-DECLARATOR spelling `int (*const ops[2])(int) = { add10, mul3 }` is a const object (C 6.7.3p9 so-qualifies the element), so its load-time-relocated slots route to relocated-read-only relro (.data.rel.ro / PE .rdata base-reloc / Mach-O __DATA dyld-rebase), NOT writable .data. The const pointer layer hides inside the parenthesized group `(*const ops[2])`; declaratorObjectIsConst now descends the group (the same recursion the type walk uses) to reach the true outermost object-forming pointer layer, so this spelling classifies like its `int *const p` / typedef'd `const op tab[N]` siblings. Shape of sqlite3.c `static int (*const sqlite3BuiltinExtensions[])(sqlite3*) = {…};`. ops[0](5)=add10(5)=15, ops[i](9)=ops[1](9)=mul3(9)=27 -> 15+27=42; a mis-relocated slot SIGSEGVs or shifts the exit off 42. `i` stays a RUNTIME index across the un-inlined pick() so the release arm keeps a real indexed load off the const table (no whole-expr const-fold). The section mis-routing is runtime-NEUTRAL (a writable const table still runs -- witnessed twice at exit-correct: c145 ELF relro + c148 PE link.exe), so the CLASSIFICATION is red-on-disable at the semantic tier (ElementConstFnPtrArrayGlobalIsConst) + the asm relocBearingGlobalSection chokepoint; this example is the runtime witness. The pe arm RUNS on windows, elf x86_64 on linux, elf arm64 under qemu, macho arm64 on darwin.
